From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

1938 annexation of Austria into Nazi Germany

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8a/Anschluss_Heldenplatz1.jpg/250px-Anschluss_Heldenplatz1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Anschluss_Heldenplatz1.jpg)

Austrian citizens gather on the [Heldenplatz](/wiki/Heldenplatz "Heldenplatz") to hear Hitler's declaration of annexation.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/State_of_Austria_within_Germany_1938.png/250px-State_of_Austria_within_Germany_1938.png)](/wiki/File:State_of_Austria_within_Germany_1938.png)

Territory of the [German Reich](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") and [Austria](/wiki/Federal_State_of_Austria "Federal State of Austria") before the Anschluss

| [Territorial evolution of Germany](/wiki/Territorial_evolution_of_Germany "Territorial evolution of Germany") in the 20th century |
| --- |

The ***Anschluss*** (German: [[ˈʔanʃlʊs]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:De-Anschluss.ogg "File:De-Anschluss.ogg"), or ***Anschluß***,[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[a]](#cite_note-2) lit. 'joining' or 'connection'), also known as the ***Anschluß Österreichs*** ([pronunciation](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/c/c9/De-Anschluss_%C3%96sterreichs.ogg/De-Anschluss_%C3%96sterreichs.ogg.mp3 "Play audio")[ⓘ](/wiki/File:De-Anschluss_%C3%96sterreichs.ogg "File:De-Anschluss Österreichs.ogg"), English: Annexation of Austria), was the [annexation](/wiki/Annexation "Annexation") of the [Federal State of Austria](/wiki/Federal_State_of_Austria "Federal State of Austria") into [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") on 12 March 1938.[[2]](#cite_note-3)

The idea of an *Anschluss* (a united Austria and Germany that would form a "[Greater Germany](/wiki/Greater_Germany "Greater Germany")")[[b]](#cite_note-5) arose after the [1871 unification of Germany](/wiki/Unification_of_Germany "Unification of Germany") excluded Austria and the German Austrians from the Prussian-dominated [German Empire](/wiki/German_Empire "German Empire"). It gained support after the [Austro-Hungarian Empire](/wiki/Austria-Hungary "Austria-Hungary") fell in 1918. The new [Republic of German-Austria](/wiki/Republic_of_German-Austria "Republic of German-Austria") attempted to form a union with Germany, but the 1919 [Treaty of Saint Germain](/wiki/Treaty_of_Saint-Germain-en-Laye_(1919) "Treaty of Saint-Germain-en-Laye (1919)") and [Treaty of Versailles](/wiki/Treaty_of_Versailles "Treaty of Versailles") forbade both the union and the continued use of the name "German-Austria" (*Deutschösterreich*); they also stripped Austria of some of its territories, such as the [Sudetenland](/wiki/Sudetenland "Sudetenland"). This left Austria without most of the territories it had ruled for centuries and amid economic crisis.

By the 1920s, the *Anschluss* proposal had strong support in both Austria and Germany,[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBukey200011-6) particularly to many Austrian citizens of the political left and center. One vehement supporter was prominent Social Democrat leader [Otto Bauer](/wiki/Otto_Bauer "Otto Bauer"), who served as Austria's Foreign Minister 21 November 1918 – 26 July 1919.[[5]](#cite_note-7)
Support for unification with Germany came mainly from the belief that Austria, stripped of its imperial land, was not viable economically.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrook-Shepherd196315-8) Popular support for the unification faded with time, although it remained as a concept in the contemporary Austrian political discourse.[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrook-Shepherd196316-9)

On 30 January 1933, [Nazi Party](/wiki/Nazi_Party "Nazi Party") leader [Adolf Hitler](/wiki/Adolf_Hitler "Adolf Hitler") was appointed chancellor of Germany. From then on, desire for unification could be identified with the [Nazi regime](/wiki/Nazi_regime "Nazi regime"), for whom it was an integral part of the Nazi "*[Heim ins Reich](/wiki/Heim_ins_Reich "Heim ins Reich")*" ("back home to the realm") concept, which sought to incorporate as many *[Volksdeutsche](/wiki/Volksdeutsche "Volksdeutsche")* (ethnic Germans outside Germany) as possible into a "[Greater Germany](/wiki/Pan-Germanism "Pan-Germanism")".[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShirer1984-10)
Nazi Germany's agents cultivated pro-unification tendencies in Austria, and sought to undermine the Austrian government, which was controlled by the [Fatherland Front](/wiki/Fatherland_Front_(Austria) "Fatherland Front (Austria)"), which opposed unification. During an [attempted coup in July 1934](/wiki/July_Putsch "July Putsch"), Austrian chancellor [Engelbert Dollfuss](/wiki/Engelbert_Dollfuss "Engelbert Dollfuss") was assassinated by Austrian Nazis. The defeat of the coup prompted many leading Austrian Nazis to go into exile in Germany, where they continued their efforts to unify the two countries.

On 5 November 1937, Hitler informed his military aides that he would annex Austria and [Czechoslovakia](/wiki/Czechoslovakia "Czechoslovakia") to the German Reich. When Austrian chancellor [Kurt Schuschnigg](/wiki/Kurt_Schuschnigg "Kurt Schuschnigg") met Hitler in [Berchtesgaden](/wiki/Berchtesgaden "Berchtesgaden") on 12 February 1938, he was presented an ultimatum and forced to appoint [Arthur Seyss-Inquart](/wiki/Arthur_Seyss-Inquart "Arthur Seyss-Inquart") as minister of the interior and security. On the eve of 9 March 1938, Schuschnigg announced that there would be a referendum to be held on 13 March to decide between a possible union with Germany or the maintenance of Austria's sovereignty. Schuschnigg expected to win a clear majority to face the Nazi challenge, but the Nazis refused and demanded the appointment of a new cabinet under Seyss-Inquart. Under the threat of military occupation, Schuschnigg resigned and Hitler had the [German Army](/wiki/German_Army_(1935%E2%80%931945) "German Army (1935–1945)") cross the border into Austria on 12 March, unopposed by the Austrian military. [A plebiscite](/wiki/1938_Austrian_Anschluss_referendum "1938 Austrian Anschluss referendum") was held on 10 April, resulting in 99.7% approval.[[9]](#cite_note-11)

## Historical background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anschluss&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Historical background")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e9/Deutscher_Bund.svg/500px-Deutscher_Bund.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Deutscher_Bund.svg)

The [German Confederation](/wiki/German_Confederation "German Confederation") 1815–1866

Main articles: [Holy Roman Empire of the German nation](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire_of_the_German_nation "Holy Roman Empire of the German nation"), [German Confederation](/wiki/German_Confederation "German Confederation"), [German question](/wiki/German_question "German question"), [German Empire](/wiki/German_Empire "German Empire"), and [German nationalism in Austria](/wiki/German_nationalism_in_Austria "German nationalism in Austria")

### Before 1918

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anschluss&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Before 1918")]

The idea of grouping all [Germans](/wiki/Ethnic_Germans "Ethnic Germans") into one nation-state had been the subject of debate in the 19th century from the [dissolution of the Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Dissolution_of_the_Holy_Roman_Empire "Dissolution of the Holy Roman Empire") in 1806 until the break-up of the [German Confederation](/wiki/German_Confederation "German Confederation") in 1866. Austria had wanted a *[Großdeutsche Lösung](/wiki/German_question "German question")* (greater Germany solution), whereby the German states would unite under the leadership of the Austrian [House of Habsburg](/wiki/House_of_Habsburg "House of Habsburg"). This solution would have included all the German states (including the non-German regions of Austria), but Prussia would have had to accept a secondary role. This controversy, called [dualism](/wiki/Austria%E2%80%93Prussia_rivalry "Austria–Prussia rivalry"), dominated [Prusso-Austrian diplomacy](/wiki/Austro-Prussian_rivalry "Austro-Prussian rivalry") and the politics of the German states in the mid-nineteenth century.[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBlackbourn1998160–175-12)

In 1866 the feud finally came to an end during the [Austro-Prussian War](/wiki/Austro-Prussian_War "Austro-Prussian War") in which the Prussians defeated the Austrians and thereby excluded the [Austrian Empire](/wiki/Austrian_Empire "Austrian Empire") and German Austrians from Germany. The Prussian statesman [Otto von Bismarck](/wiki/Otto_von_Bismarck "Otto von Bismarck") formed the [North German Confederation](/wiki/North_German_Confederation "North German Confederation"), which included most of the remaining German states, aside from a few in the southwestern region of the German-inhabited lands, and further expanded the power of the [Kingdom of Prussia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Prussia "Kingdom of Prussia"). Bismarck used the [Franco-Prussian War](/wiki/Franco-Prussian_War "Franco-Prussian War") (1870–1871) as a way to convince southwestern German states, including the [Kingdom of Bavaria](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Bavaria "Kingdom of Bavaria"), to side with Prussia against the [Second French Empire](/wiki/Second_French_Empire "Second French Empire"). Due to Prussia's [quick victory](/wiki/Battle_of_Sedan "Battle of Sedan"), the debate was settled and in 1871 the "*Kleindeutsch*" [German Empire](/wiki/German_Empire "German Empire") based on the leadership of Bismarck and Prussia formed—this excluded Austria.[[11]](#cite_note-13) Besides ensuring Prussian domination of a [united Germany](/wiki/Unification_of_Germany "Unification of Germany"), the exclusion of Austria also ensured that Germany would have a substantial [Protestant](/wiki/Protestantism_in_Germany "Protestantism in Germany") majority. The exclusion of the Austrian Germans and Austria from Germany prompted the [Social Democratic Party of Austria](/wiki/Social_Democratic_Party_of_Austria "Social Democratic Party of Austria")’s leader [Otto Bauer](/wiki/Otto_Bauer "Otto Bauer") to state that the dilemma was "the conflict between our Austrian and German character.”[[12]](#cite_note-14)

The [Austro-Hungarian Compromise of 1867](/wiki/Austro-Hungarian_Compromise_of_1867 "Austro-Hungarian Compromise of 1867"), the *Ausgleich*, provided for a dual sovereignty, the [Austrian Empire](/wiki/Austrian_Empire "Austrian Empire") and the [Kingdom of Hungary](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Hungary "Kingdom of Hungary"), under [Franz Joseph I](/wiki/Franz_Joseph_I_of_Austria "Franz Joseph I of Austria"). This diverse empire included various different ethnic groups including Hungarians, Slavic ethnic groups such as Bosniaks,[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBougarel201816–17-15) Croats, Czechs, Poles, Rusyns, Serbs, Slovaks, Slovenes, and Ukrainians, as well as Italians and Romanians ruled by a German minority.[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETaylor199025-16) The empire caused tensions between the various ethnic groups. Many Austrian pan-Germans showed loyalty to [Otto von Bismarck](/wiki/Otto_von_Bismarck "Otto von Bismarck")[[15]](#cite_note-17) and only to Germany, wore symbols that were temporarily banned in Austrian schools and advocated the dissolution of the empire to allow Austria to rejoin Germany, as it had been during the German Confederation of 1815–1866.[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUnowsky2005157-18)[[17]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGiloi2011161–162-19) Although many Austrians supported pan-Germanism, many others still showed allegiance to the [Habsburg monarchy](/wiki/Habsburg_monarchy "Habsburg monarchy") and wished for Austria to remain an independent country.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELow197414–16-20)

### Aftermath of World War I

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anschluss&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Aftermath of World War I")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/50/Dissolution_of_Austria-Hungary.png/500px-Dissolution_of_Austria-Hungary.png)](/wiki/File:Dissolution_of_Austria-Hungary.png)

Map of the dissolution of [Austria-Hungary](/wiki/Austria-Hungary "Austria-Hungary") in 1918

[Erich Ludendorff](/wiki/Erich_Ludendorff "Erich Ludendorff") wrote to the [Federal Foreign Office](/wiki/Federal_Foreign_Office "Federal Foreign Office") on 14 October 1918 about the possibility of conducting an Anschluss with the German areas of [Austria-Hungary](/wiki/Austria-Hungary "Austria-Hungary") as its [dissolution](/wiki/Dissolution_of_Austria-Hungary "Dissolution of Austria-Hungary") removed the problem of the country's numerous ethnic groups. Secretary [Wilhelm Solf](/wiki/Wilhelm_Solf "Wilhelm Solf") opposed the proposal, stating that it "would provide the [Entente](/wiki/Triple_Entente "Triple Entente") with justification for demanding territorial compensations". During the [Paris Peace Conference](/wiki/Paris_Peace_Conference_(1919%E2%80%931920) "Paris Peace Conference (1919–1920)") the French sought to forbid a union between Austria and Germany, with French Minister of Foreign Affairs [Stephen Pichon](/wiki/Stephen_Pichon "Stephen Pichon") stating that they "must see that Germany is not given an opportunity to rebuild her strength by utilizing the Austrian populations which remain outside of Czechoslovakia, Poland, and Jugoslavia". A compromise was reached and Article 80 of the [Treaty of Versailles](/wiki/Treaty_of_Versailles "Treaty of Versailles") stated that "Germany acknowledges and will respect strictly the independence of Austria, within the frontiers which may be fixed in a Treaty between that State and the Principal Allied and Associated Powers; she agrees that this independence shall be inalienable, except with the consent of the Council of the [League of Nations](/wiki/League_of_Nations "League of Nations")."[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGehl19631–2-21)

Elite and popular opinion in the rump [Republic of German-Austria](/wiki/Republic_of_German-Austria "Republic of German-Austria") after 1918 largely favored some sort of union with Germany.[[20]](#cite_note-22) An Austrian provisional national assembly drafted a provisional constitution that stated that "German Austria is a democratic republic" (Article 1) and "German Austria is a component of the German Republic" (Article 2). Later plebiscites in the Austrian border provinces of [Tyrol](/wiki/Tyrol_(state) "Tyrol (state)") and [Salzburg](/wiki/Salzburg "Salzburg") yielded majorities of 98% and 99% in favor of a unification with the [Weimar Republic](/wiki/Weimar_Republic "Weimar Republic"). Further plebiscites were then forbidden. However, [Erich Bielka](/wiki/Erich_Bielka "Erich Bielka") noted that the plebiscites were marred by electoral fraud and voter manipulation, and therefore did not reflect what the general Austrian opinion was at that time:[[21]](#cite_note-manning-23)[[22]](#cite_note-bielka-24)

> In addition to the massive propaganda campaign and not insignificant Reich German influence, 'Ja' ballot papers were pre-printed and provided at the polling stations and ballots were to be handed to an election official, undermining voter confidentiality. In addition, voter eligibility rules were liberally conceived and, therefore, open to abuse. Not only were those registered for the Nationalrat elections of October 1920 permitted to vote, but also those who registered themselves as living in Tyrol before April 1921, that is, less than a fortnight before going to the polls, as were all those Tyroleans who lived outside of the state; a train was even chartered from Bavaria to mitigate the financial burden of travelling 'home'.[[21]](#cite_note-manning-23)

In the aftermath of a prohibition of an Anschluss, Germans in both Austria and Germany pointed to a contradiction in the national [self-determination](/wiki/Self-determination "Self-determination") principle because the treaties failed to grant self-determination to the ethnic Germans (such as German Austrians and [Sudeten Germans](/wiki/Sudeten_Germans "Sudeten Germans")) outside of the German Reich.[[23]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStackelberg1999194-25)[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELow19767-26) [Hugo Preuss](/wiki/Hugo_Preuss "Hugo Preuss"), the drafter of the German [Weimar Constitution](/wiki/Weimar_Constitution "Weimar Constitution"), criticized efforts to prevent an Anschluss; he saw the prohibition as a contradiction of the [Wilsonian](/wiki/Wilsonian "Wilsonian") principle of self-determination of peoples.[[25]](#cite_note-27)

The constitutions of the [Weimar Republic](/wiki/Weimar_Republic "Weimar Republic") and the [First Austrian Republic](/wiki/First_Austrian_Republic "First Austrian Republic") both included the political goal of unification, which parties widely supported. In the early 1930s, the Austrian government looked to a possible [customs union](/wiki/Customs_union "Customs union") with the German Republic in 1931. However, ultimately regional patriotism was stronger than pan-German sentiment.[[21]](#cite_note-manning-23) In the Austrian Empire, each [Kronland](/wiki/Crown_land#Austria "Crown land") had its own functional government and enjoyed a fair amount of autonomy from [Vienna](/wiki/Vienna "Vienna"), with "each looking to their own capital" instead.[[21]](#cite_note-manning-23) According to Jody Manning, the idea of unification with Germany was not overwhelmingly popular among the Austrian population in 1919, which is one of the reasons why no nationwide referendum was held, even before it was forbidden by the Entente:

> Despite the initially compelling statistics, overall, it appears doubtful that a qualified majority of Austrians would have supported Anschluss with Germany. From the sparse evidence available, it appears that the pro-Anschluss movement could only hope for a slim majority in the event of a plebiscite, and not the 75 per cent necessary, and that the number of Anschluss supporters in 1919 was not more than 50 per cent of the population. Even Otto Bauer, leader of the Social Democratic party, had to admit that both the [bourgeoisie](/wiki/Bourgeoisie "Bourgeoisie") and the peasantry wanted 'an independent Austria fully capable of a national life of its own'. Also telling is Bauer's admission that, because of the strength of the conservative opposition to Anschluss and the real possibility that the majority would have voted against the Anschluss, the Socialists did not dare to hold a referendum in 1919.[[21]](#cite_note-manning-23)[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGould1950228–229-28)

[Poincarés third government](/wiki/Raymond_Poincar%C3%A9#Third_premiership "Raymond Poincaré") attempted to prevent an Anschluss by incorporating Austria into a Danubian Confederation in 1927. German Minister of Foreign Affairs [Gustav Stresemann](/wiki/Gustav_Stresemann "Gustav Stresemann") opposed it, as he saw it as an attempt to re-form the Austro-Hungarian Empire and offered to form a [customs union](/wiki/Customs_union "Customs union") with Austria. Austrian Chancellor [Ignaz Seipel](/wiki/Ignaz_Seipel "Ignaz Seipel"), an Anschluss opponent, rejected the offer. In September 1929, Seipel was replaced by [Johannes Schober](/wiki/Johannes_Schober "Johannes Schober"), who pursued a pro-Germany policy and attempted to form a customs union. A political and [economical crisis](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression") led to Schober losing power (30 September 1930) and Seipel returning to the government as Minister of Foreign Affairs. Negotiations were restarted after [Otto Ender](/wiki/Otto_Ender "Otto Ender") became chancellor and were finalized with German Foreign Affairs Minister [Julius Curtius](/wiki/Julius_Curtius "Julius Curtius") on 5 March 1931, before being approved by Germany on 18 March. France opposed the customs union, stating that it was in violation of Article 88 of the [Treaty of Saint-Germain-en-Laye](/wiki/Treaty_of_Saint-Germain-en-Laye_(1919) "Treaty of Saint-Germain-en-Laye (1919)").[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGehl19634–8-29)

### Nazi Germany, Fascist Italy, and Austria

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anschluss&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Nazi Germany, Fascist Italy, and Austria")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/21/Deutsche_Milit%C3%A4rkarte_Schweiz-Norditalien.png/500px-Deutsche_Milit%C3%A4rkarte_Schweiz-Norditalien.png)](/wiki/File:Deutsche_Milit%C3%A4rkarte_Schweiz-Norditalien.png)

German military map during the Second World War, with no border between Germany and Austria (top right; also showing Alsace as part of Germany because it was directly incorporated into the Reich)

When the [Nazis](/wiki/Nazi_Party "Nazi Party"), led by [Adolf Hitler](/wiki/Adolf_Hitler "Adolf Hitler"), rose to power in the Weimar Republic, the Austrian government withdrew from economic ties. Like Germany, Austria experienced the economic turbulence which was a result of the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression"), with a high unemployment rate, and unstable commerce and industry. During the 1920s it was a target for German investment capital. By 1937, rapid [German rearmament](/wiki/German_re-armament "German re-armament") increased Berlin's interest in annexing Austria, rich in raw materials and labour. It supplied Germany with [magnesium](/wiki/Magnesium "Magnesium") and the products of the iron, textile and machine industries. It had gold and foreign currency reserves, many unemployed skilled workers, hundreds of idle factories, and large potential hydroelectric resources.[[28]](#cite_note-30)

Hitler, an Austrian German by birth,[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETaylor2001257-31)[[c]](#cite_note-33) picked up his [German nationalist](/wiki/Pan-Germanism "Pan-Germanism") ideas at a young age. Whilst infiltrating the [German Workers' Party](/wiki/German_Workers%27_Party "German Workers' Party") (DAP), Hitler became involved in a heated political argument with a visitor, a [Professor Baumann](/wiki/Adalbert_Baumann "Adalbert Baumann"), who proposed that [Bavaria](/wiki/Bavaria "Bavaria") should break away from [Prussia](/wiki/Prussia "Prussia") and found a new [South German](/wiki/Southern_Germany "Southern Germany") nation with Austria. In vehemently attacking the man's arguments he made an impression on the other party members with his oratorical skills and, according to Hitler, the "professor" left the hall acknowledging unequivocal defeat.[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKershaw200875-34) Impressed with Hitler, [Anton Drexler](/wiki/Anton_Drexler "Anton Drexler") invited him to join the DAP. Hitler accepted on 12 September 1919,[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStackelberg20079-35) becoming the party's 55th member.[[33]](#cite_note-36) After becoming leader of the DAP, Hitler addressed a crowd on 24 February 1920, and in an effort to appeal to wider parts of the German population, the DAP was renamed the [National Socialist German Workers' Party](/wiki/National_Socialist_German_Workers%27_Party "National Socialist German Workers' Party") (NSDAP).[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKershaw200887-37)

As its first point, the 1920 [National Socialist Program](/wiki/National_Socialist_Program "National Socialist Program") stated, "We demand the unification of all Germans in the Greater Germany on the basis of the people's right to self-determination. Hitler argued in a 1921 essay that the [German Reich](/wiki/German_Empire "German Empire") had a single task of, "incorporating the ten million German-Austrians in the Empire and dethroning the Habsburgs, the most miserable dynasty ever ruling."[[35]](#cite_note-38) The [Nazis](/wiki/Nazism "Nazism") aimed to [re-unite all Germans](/wiki/Heim_ins_Reich "Heim ins Reich") who were either born in the *Reich* or living outside it in order to create an "all-German *Reich*". Hitler wrote in *[Mein Kampf](/wiki/Mein_Kampf "Mein Kampf")* (1925) that he would create a union between his birth country Austria and Germany by any means possible.[[36]](#cite_note-Hitler2010-39)[*[non-primary source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:No_original_research#Primary,_secondary_and_tertiary_sources "Wikipedia:No original research")*]

The [First Austrian Republic](/wiki/First_Austrian_Republic "First Austrian Republic") was dominated from the late 1920s by the [Christian Social Party](/wiki/Christian_Social_Party_(Austria) "Christian Social Party (Austria)") (CS), whose economic policies were based on the papal encyclical *[Rerum novarum](/wiki/Rerum_novarum "Rerum novarum")*. The First Republic gradually disintegrated in 1933, when parliament was dissolved and power was centralized in the office of the [chancellor](/wiki/Chancellor_of_Austria "Chancellor of Austria"), who was empowered to [rule by decree](/wiki/Rule_by_decree "Rule by decree"). Rival parties, including the Austrian National Socialists, were banned, and government evolved into a [corporatist](/wiki/Corporatist "Corporatist"), one-party government that combined the CS and the paramilitary *[Heimwehr](/wiki/Heimwehr "Heimwehr")*. It controlled labor relations and the press. (*See [Fatherland Front](/wiki/Fatherland_Front_(Austria) "Fatherland Front (Austria)")*).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The new regime emphasized the Catholic elements of Austria's national identity and staunchly opposed union with [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany").

[Engelbert Dollfuss](/wiki/Engelbert_Dollfuss "Engelbert Dollfuss") and his successor, [Kurt Schuschnigg](/wiki/Kurt_Schuschnigg "Kurt Schuschnigg"), turned to [Benito Mussolini](/wiki/Benito_Mussolini "Benito Mussolini")'s [Italy](/wiki/Fascist_Italy_(1922%E2%80%931943) "Fascist Italy (1922–1943)") for inspiration and support. Mussolini supported the independence of Austria, largely due to his concern that Hitler would eventually press for the return of Italian territories which had once been ruled by Austria. The pro-Austrian policy of Italy was such that it was sometimes compared that while a century before (pre-unification) Italy was Austria's puppet, now rump state Austria was Italy's, and Mussolini mobilised Italian troops sent to the [Brenner Pass](/wiki/Brenner_Pass "Brenner Pass") after the attempted coup that included the murder of Dollfuss in 1934. Afterwards Italy had formed a common front with Great Britain and France opposed to German rearmament in early 1935 in the context of German withdrawal from the International Disarmament Conference, the [Stresa Front](/wiki/Stresa_Front "Stresa Front").[[37]](#cite_note-40)

However, the Stresa Front collapsed after French foreign ministry signed a [pact with the USSR](/wiki/Franco-Soviet_Pact "Franco-Soviet Pact") in early 1935, and Britain reacted by signing a [naval agreement](/wiki/Anglo-German_Naval_Agreement "Anglo-German Naval Agreement") with Germany. As such, when the Stresa Pact ministers’ ([Samuel Hoare](/wiki/Samuel_Hoare,_1st_Viscount_Templewood "Samuel Hoare, 1st Viscount Templewood") for Great Britain and [Pierre Laval](/wiki/Pierre_Laval "Pierre Laval") for France) [attempt to appease Italy](/wiki/Hoare-Laval_Pact "Hoare-Laval Pact") with part of Ethiopia after Mussolini's invasion in October 1935 was leaked to an indignant press and thereby retracted, Mussolini now needed support elsewhere in Ethiopia (*see [Second Italo-Abyssinian War](/wiki/Second_Italo-Abyssinian_War "Second Italo-Abyssinian War")*), and from Germany he had it readily. After receiving [Hitler's personal assurance](/wiki/South_Tyrol_Option_Agreement "South Tyrol Option Agreement") that Germany would not seek territorial concessions from Italy, Mussolini entered into a relationship with Berlin that began with the formation of the [Berlin–Rome Axis](/wiki/Axis_Powers "Axis Powers") in 1936 which was formalised into a military alliance in the [Pact of Steel](/wiki/Pact_of_Steel "Pact of Steel") in 1939. By 1938 there was little Italian dismay at the annexation of Austria given her preoccupation in the Spanish Civil War and relative reconciliation with Germany, especially with pro-German foreign minister Count [Galeazzo Ciano](/wiki/Galeazzo_Ciano "Galeazzo Ciano") in office, whose appointment in 1936 is cited as the “death-knell for independent Austria”.[[38]](#cite_note-41)

### Austrian Civil War to *Anschluss*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anschluss&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Austrian Civil War to Anschluss")]

Main articles: [Austrian Civil War](/wiki/Austrian_Civil_War "Austrian Civil War"), [July Putsch](/wiki/July_Putsch "July Putsch"), and [Federal State of Austria](/wiki/Federal_State_of_Austria "Federal State of Austria")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/Bundesarchiv_Bild_102-00805%2C_Wien%2C_Februark%C3%A4mpfe%2C_Bundesheer_2.jpg/500px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_102-00805%2C_Wien%2C_Februark%C3%A4mpfe%2C_Bundesheer_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_102-00805,_Wien,_Februark%C3%A4mpfe,_Bundesheer_2.jpg)

Soldiers of the [Austrian Federal Army](/wiki/Austrian_Federal_Army "Austrian Federal Army") in Vienna, 12 February 1934

The [Austrian Nazi Party](/wiki/Austrian_Nazi_Party "Austrian Nazi Party") failed to win any seats in the [November 1930 general election](/wiki/1930_Austrian_legislative_election "1930 Austrian legislative election"), but its popularity grew in Austria after Hitler came to power in Germany. The idea of the country joining Germany also grew in popularity, thanks in part to a [Nazi propaganda](/wiki/Nazi_propaganda "Nazi propaganda") campaign which used slogans such as *[Ein Volk, ein Reich, ein Führer](/wiki/Ein_Volk,_ein_Reich,_ein_F%C3%BChrer "Ein Volk, ein Reich, ein Führer")* ("One People, One Empire, One Leader") to try to convince Austrians to advocate for an *Anschluss* to the German Reich.[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZeman1973137–142-42) *Anschluss* might have occurred by democratic process had Austrian Nazis not begun a terrorism campaign. According to [John Gunther](/wiki/John_Gunther "John Gunther") in 1936, "In 1932 Austria was probably eighty percent pro-*Anschluss*".[[40]](#cite_note-gunther1936-43)

When Germany permitted residents of Austria to vote[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] on 5 March 1933, three special trains, boats and trucks brought such masses to [Passau](/wiki/Passau "Passau") that the [SS](/wiki/Schutzstaffel "Schutzstaffel") staged a ceremonial welcome.[[41]](#cite_note-44) Gunther wrote that by the end of 1933 Austrian public opinion about German annexation was at least 60% against.[[40]](#cite_note-gunther1936-43) On 25 July 1934, chancellor Dollfuss was assassinated by Austrian Nazis in a failed coup. Afterwards, leading Austrian Nazis fled to Germany but they continued to push for unification from there. The remaining Austrian Nazis continued terrorist attacks against Austrian governmental institutions, which caused 164 deaths and 636 injuries between 1933 and 1938.[[42]](#cite_note-45)

Dollfuss's successor was [Kurt Schuschnigg](/wiki/Kurt_Schuschnigg "Kurt Schuschnigg"), who followed a political course similar to his predecessor. In 1935 Schuschnigg used the police to suppress Nazi supporters. Police actions under Schuschnigg included gathering Nazis (and Social Democrats) and holding them in [internment camps](/wiki/Internment_camp "Internment camp"). Austria between 1934 and 1938 focused on the history of Austria and opposed the absorption of Austria into Nazi Germany (according to the philosophy Austrians were "superior Germans"). Schuschnigg called Austria the "better German state" but struggled to keep Austria independent.

In an attempt to put Schuschnigg's mind at rest, Hitler delivered a speech at the [Reichstag](/wiki/Reichstag_(Nazi_Germany) "Reichstag (Nazi Germany)") and said, "Germany neither intends nor wishes to interfere in the internal affairs of Austria, to annex Austria or to conclude an Anschluss."[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShirer1990296-46)

In summer 1936, Schuschnigg told Mussolini that his country had to come to an agreement with Germany. On 11 July 1936 he signed an agreement with German ambassador [Franz von Papen](/wiki/Franz_von_Papen "Franz von Papen"), in which Schuschnigg agreed to the release of Nazis imprisoned in Austria and Germany promised to respect Austrian sovereignty.[[40]](#cite_note-gunther1936-43) Under the terms of the Austro-German treaty, Austria declared itself a "German state" that would always follow Germany's lead in foreign policy, and members of the "National Opposition" were allowed to enter the cabinet, in exchange for which the Austrian Nazis promised to cease their terrorist attacks against the government. This did not satisfy Hitler and the pro-German Austrian Nazis grew in strength.

In September 1936, Hitler launched the [Four Year Plan](/wiki/Four_Year_Plan "Four Year Plan") that called for a dramatic increase in military spending and to make Germany as [autarkic](/wiki/Autarky "Autarky") as possible with the aim of having the *Reich* ready to fight a world war by 1940.[[44]](#cite_note-overy-47) The Four Year Plan required huge investments in the *[Reichswerke](/wiki/Reichswerke_Hermann_G%C3%B6ring "Reichswerke Hermann Göring")* steel works, a programme for developing [synthetic oil](/wiki/Synthetic_oil "Synthetic oil") that soon went wildly over budget, and programmes for producing more chemicals and aluminium; the plan called for a policy of substituting imports and rationalizing industry to achieve its goals that failed completely.[[44]](#cite_note-overy-47) As the Four Year Plan fell further and further behind its targets, [Hermann Göring](/wiki/Hermann_G%C3%B6ring "Hermann Göring"), the chief of the Four Year Plan office, began to press for an *Anschluss* as a way of securing Austria's iron and other raw materials as a solution to the problems with the Four Year Plan.[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKershaw200167-48) The British historian [Ian Kershaw](/wiki/Ian_Kershaw "Ian Kershaw") wrote:

> [A]bove all, it was Hermann Göring, at this time close to the pinnacle of his power, who far more than Hitler, throughout 1937 made the running and pushed the hardest for an early and radical solution to the 'Austrian Question'. Göring was not simply operating as Hitler's agent in matters relating to the 'Austrian Question'. His approach differed in emphasis in significant respects...But Göring's broad notions of foreign policy, which he pushed to a great extent on his own initiative in the mid-1930s drew more on traditional pan-German concepts of nationalist power-politics to attain hegemony in Europe than on the racial dogmatism central to Hitler's ideology.[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKershaw200167-48)

Göring was far more interested in the return of the former [German colonies](/wiki/German_colonial_empire "German colonial empire") in [Africa](/wiki/Africa "Africa") than Hitler was, believing up to 1939 in the possibility of an Anglo-German alliance (an idea that Hitler had abandoned by late 1937), and wanted all Eastern Europe in the German economic [sphere of influence](/wiki/Sphere_of_influence "Sphere of influence").[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKershaw200167–68-49) Göring did not share Hitler's interest in *[Lebensraum](/wiki/Lebensraum "Lebensraum")* ("living space") as for him, merely having Eastern Europe in the German economic sphere of influence was sufficient.[[45]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKershaw200167-48) In this context, having Austria annexed to Germany was the key towards bringing Eastern Europe into Göring's desired *Grossraumwirtschaft* ("greater economic space").[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKershaw200167–68-49)

Faced with problems in the Four Year Plan, Göring had become the loudest voice in Germany, calling for an *Anschluss*, even at the risk of losing an alliance with Italy.[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKershaw200168-50) In April 1937, in a secret speech before a group of German industrialists, Göring stated that the only solution to the problems with meeting the steel production targets laid out by the Four Year Plan was to annex Austria, which Göring noted was rich in iron.[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKershaw200168-50) Göring did not give a date for the *Anschluss*, but given that Four Year Plan's targets all had to be met by September 1940, and the current problems with meeting the steel production targets, suggested that he wanted an *Anschluss* in the very near-future.[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKershaw200168-50)

## End of an independent Austria

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anschluss&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: End of an independent Austria")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dd/1clerofascismo1.jpg/250px-1clerofascismo1.jpg)](/wiki/File:1clerofascismo1.jpg)

Supporters of Schuschnigg campaigning for the independence of Austria in March 1938, shortly before the *Anschluss*

Hitler told Goebbels in the late summer of 1937 that eventually Austria would have to be taken "by force".[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKershaw200145-51) On 5 November 1937, Hitler called a meeting with the Foreign Minister [Konstantin von Neurath](/wiki/Konstantin_von_Neurath "Konstantin von Neurath"), the War Minister Field Marshal [Werner von Blomberg](/wiki/Werner_von_Blomberg "Werner von Blomberg"), the Army commander General [Werner von Fritsch](/wiki/Werner_von_Fritsch "Werner von Fritsch"), the [Kriegsmarine](/wiki/Kriegsmarine "Kriegsmarine") commander Admiral [Erich Raeder](/wiki/Erich_Raeder "Erich Raeder") and the [Luftwaffe](/wiki/Luftwaffe "Luftwaffe") commander Hermann Göring recorded in the [Hossbach Memorandum](/wiki/Hossbach_Memorandum "Hossbach Memorandum"). At the conference, Hitler stated that economic problems were causing Germany to fall behind in the arms race with Britain and France, and that the only solution was to launch in the near-future a series of wars to seize Austria and [Czechoslovakia](/wiki/First_Czechoslovak_Republic "First Czechoslovak Republic"), whose economies would be plundered to give Germany the lead in the arms race.[[49]](#cite_note-52)[[50]](#cite_note-53) In early 1938, Hitler was seriously considering replacing Papen as ambassador to Austria with either Colonel [Hermann Kriebel](/wiki/Hermann_Kriebel "Hermann Kriebel"), the German consul in [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai "Shanghai"), or [Albert Forster](/wiki/Albert_Forster "Albert Forster"), the *[Gauleiter](/wiki/Gauleiter "Gauleiter")* of Danzig.[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeinberg198146-54) Significantly, neither Kriebel nor Forster was a professional diplomat, with Kriebel being one of the leaders of the [1923 Munich Beerhall *putsch*](/wiki/Beer_Hall_Putsch "Beer Hall Putsch") who had been appointed consul in Shanghai to facilitate his work as an arms dealer in China, while Forster was a *Gauleiter* who had proven he could get along with the Poles in his position in the [Free City of Danzig](/wiki/Free_City_of_Danzig "Free City of Danzig"); both men were Nazis who had shown some diplomatic skill.[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeinberg198146-54) On 25 January 1938, the Austrian police raided the Vienna headquarters of the Austrian Nazi Party, arresting *Gauleiter* Leopold Tavs, the deputy to Captain [Josef Leopold](/wiki/Josef_Leopold "Josef Leopold"), discovered a cache of arms and plans for a *putsch*.[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeinberg198146-54)

Following increasing violence and demands from Hitler that Austria agree to a union, Schuschnigg met Hitler at [Berchtesgaden](/wiki/Berchtesgaden "Berchtesgaden") on 12 February 1938, in an attempt to avoid the takeover of Austria. Hitler presented Schuschnigg with a set of demands including appointing Nazi sympathizers to positions of power in the government. The key appointment was that of [Arthur Seyss-Inquart](/wiki/Arthur_Seyss-Inquart "Arthur Seyss-Inquart") as Minister of Public Security, with full, unlimited control of the police. In return Hitler would publicly reaffirm the treaty of 11 July 1936 and reaffirm his support for Austria's national sovereignty. Browbeaten and threatened by Hitler, Schuschnigg agreed to these demands and put them into effect.[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFaber2009108–118-55)

Seyss-Inquart was a long-time supporter of the Nazis who sought the union of all Germans in one state. Leopold argues he was a moderate who favoured an evolutionary approach to union. He opposed the violent tactics of the Austrian Nazis, cooperated with Catholic groups, and wanted to preserve a measure of Austrian identity within Nazi Germany.[[53]](#cite_note-56)

On 20 February, Hitler made a speech before the Reichstag which was broadcast live and which for the first time was relayed also by the [Austrian radio network](/wiki/ORF_(broadcaster) "ORF (broadcaster)"). A key phrase in the speech which was aimed at the Germans living in Austria and Czechoslovakia was: "The German Reich is no longer willing to tolerate the suppression of ten million Germans across its borders."[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMacDonogh200935-57)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1a/Engelbert_Dollfuss.png/250px-Engelbert_Dollfuss.png)](/wiki/File:Engelbert_Dollfuss.png)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/Kurt_Schuschnigg_1934.jpg/250px-Kurt_Schuschnigg_1934.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kurt_Schuschnigg_1934.jpg)

The [Dollfuss](/wiki/Engelbert_Dollfuss "Engelbert Dollfuss")/[Schuschnigg](/wiki/Kurt_Schuschnigg "Kurt Schuschnigg") regime fought to keep Austria as an independent country.

### Schuschnigg announces a referendum

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anschluss&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Schuschnigg announces a referendum")]

On 3 March 1938, Austrian Socialists offered to back Schuschnigg's government in exchange for political concessions, such as legalising socialist press, returning confiscated funds and "the lifting of the ban on the wearing of Social Democrat badges, show Social Democrat flags and standards and singing Social Democrat songs."[[55]](#cite_note-58) Schuschnigg agreed to these demands and was supported by the united front of socialists and communists, as well as the Heimwehr, monarchist groups and the majority of the Austrian police. The Social Democrats also declared their readiness to support Schuschnigg in the event of a plebiscite under the conditions that immediately after such a plebiscite a definite negotiation be begun to include them in the Government.[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKnaur1951367–369-59) This support led Schuschnigg to announce the referendum. According to Peter R. Knaur, Germany believed that a defeat in a referendum was likely, and thus dispatched men to Vienna to prevent or modify the plebiscite. Knaur wrote: "The Nazis supposedly admitted that they only had the following of twenty percent, of the potential votes of the country."[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKnaur1951370-60)

On 9 March 1938, in the face of rioting by the small, but virulent, Austrian Nazi Party and ever-expanding German demands on Austria, Chancellor [Kurt Schuschnigg](/wiki/Kurt_Schuschnigg "Kurt Schuschnigg") called a [referendum](/wiki/Referendum "Referendum") (plebiscite) on the issue, to be held on 13 March. Infuriated, on 11 March, Adolf Hitler threatened invasion of Austria, and demanded Chancellor von Schuschnigg's resignation and the appointment of the Nazi [Arthur Seyss-Inquart](/wiki/Arthur_Seyss-Inquart "Arthur Seyss-Inquart") as his replacement. Hitler's plan was for Seyss-Inquart to call immediately for German troops to rush to Austria's aid, restoring order and giving the invasion an air of legitimacy. In the face of this threat, Schuschnigg informed Seyss-Inquart that the plebiscite would be cancelled.

To secure a large majority in the referendum, Schuschnigg dismantled the one-party state. He agreed to legalize the [Social Democrats](/wiki/Social_Democratic_Party_of_Austria "Social Democratic Party of Austria") and their trade unions in return for their support in the referendum.[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShirer1984-10) He also set the minimum voting age at 24 to exclude younger voters because the Nazi movement was most popular among the young.[[58]](#cite_note-61) In contrast, Hitler had lowered the voting age for German elections held under Nazi rule, largely to compensate for the removal of [Jews](/wiki/German_Jews "German Jews") and other ethnic minorities from the German electorate following enactment of the [Nuremberg Laws](/wiki/Nuremberg_Laws "Nuremberg Laws") in 1935.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The plan went awry when it became apparent that Hitler would not stand by while Austria declared its independence by public vote. Hitler declared that the referendum would be subject to major fraud and that Germany would never accept it. In addition, the German ministry of propaganda issued press reports that riots had broken out in Austria and that large parts of the Austrian population were calling for German troops to restore order. Schuschnigg immediately responded that reports of riots were false.[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFaber2009121–124-62)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4b/Bundesarchiv_Bild_119-5243%2C_Wien%2C_Arthur_Sey%C3%9F-Inquart%2C_Adolf_Hitler.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_119-5243%2C_Wien%2C_Arthur_Sey%C3%9F-Inquart%2C_Adolf_Hitler.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_119-5243,_Wien,_Arthur_Sey%C3%9F-Inquart,_Adolf_Hitler.jpg)

[Seyss-Inquart](/wiki/Arthur_Seyss-Inquart "Arthur Seyss-Inquart") and Hitler with Himmler and Heydrich to the right in Vienna, March 1938

Hitler sent an ultimatum to Schuschnigg on 11 March, demanding that he hand over all power to the Austrian Nazis or face an invasion. The ultimatum was set to expire at noon, but was extended by two hours. Without waiting for an answer, Hitler had already signed the order to send troops into Austria at one o'clock.[[60]](#cite_note-:0-63)
Nevertheless, the German Führer underestimated his opposition.

As journalist [Edgar Ansel Mowrer](/wiki/Edgar_Ansel_Mowrer "Edgar Ansel Mowrer"), reporting from Paris for [CBS News](/wiki/CBS_News "CBS News"), observed: "There is no one in all France who does not believe that Hitler invaded Austria not to hold a genuine plebiscite, but to prevent the plebiscite planned by Schuschnigg from demonstrating to the entire world just how little hold National Socialism really had on that tiny country."[[61]](#cite_note-otr.com-64)

Schuschnigg desperately sought support for Austrian independence in the hours following the ultimatum. Realizing that neither France nor Britain was willing to offer assistance, Schuschnigg resigned on the evening of 11 March, but President [Wilhelm Miklas](/wiki/Wilhelm_Miklas "Wilhelm Miklas") refused to appoint Seyss-Inquart as Chancellor. At 8:45 pm, Hitler, tired of waiting, ordered the invasion to commence at dawn on 12 March regardless.[[62]](#cite_note-65) Around 10 pm, a forged telegram was sent in Seyss-Inquart's name asking for German troops, since he was not yet Chancellor and was unable to do so himself. Seyss-Inquart was not installed as Chancellor until after midnight, when Miklas resigned himself to the inevitable.[[60]](#cite_note-:0-63)[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShirer1984-10) In the radio broadcast in which Schuschnigg announced his resignation, he argued that he accepted the changes and allowed the Nazis to take over the government "to avoid the shedding of fraternal blood [*Bruderblut*]".[[63]](#cite_note-66) Seyss-Inquart was appointed chancellor after midnight on 12 March.

It is said that after listening to [Bruckner's Seventh Symphony](/wiki/Symphony_No._7_(Bruckner) "Symphony No. 7 (Bruckner)"), Hitler cried: "How can anyone say that Austria is not German! Is there anything more German than our old pure Austrianness?"[[64]](#cite_note-67)

### German troops march into Austria

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anschluss&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: German troops march into Austria")]

Main article: [Austria within Nazi Germany](/wiki/Austria_within_Nazi_Germany "Austria within Nazi Germany")

Clip from UFA newsreel "German Entry into Austria"

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5c/Bundesarchiv_Bild_146-1985-083-10%2C_Anschluss_%C3%96sterreich%2C_Wien.jpg/330px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_146-1985-083-10%2C_Anschluss_%C3%96sterreich%2C_Wien.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_146-1985-083-10,_Anschluss_%C3%96sterreich,_Wien.jpg)

Cheering crowds greet the Nazis in Vienna.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/Hitler_Crosses_into_Austria_in_1938.jpg/330px-Hitler_Crosses_into_Austria_in_1938.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hitler_Crosses_into_Austria_in_1938.jpg)

Hitler crosses the border into Austria in March 1938.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-1987-0922-500%2C_Wien%2C_Heldenplatz%2C_Rede_Adolf_Hitler.jpg/330px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-1987-0922-500%2C_Wien%2C_Heldenplatz%2C_Rede_Adolf_Hitler.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_183-1987-0922-500,_Wien,_Heldenplatz,_Rede_Adolf_Hitler.jpg)

Hitler announces the *Anschluss* on the [Heldenplatz](/wiki/Heldenplatz "Heldenplatz"), Vienna, 15 March 1938.

On the morning of 12 March 1938, the [8th Army](/wiki/8th_Army_(Wehrmacht) "8th Army (Wehrmacht)") of the German *[Wehrmacht](/wiki/Wehrmacht "Wehrmacht")* crossed the border into Austria. The troops were greeted by cheering Austrians with Nazi salutes, Nazi flags, and flowers.[[d]](#cite_note-69) The "invasion" without shots fired was therefore dubbed the Blumenkrieg ("Flower War"). Some contemporary observers doubted the authenticity of this ecstatic welcome: [Friedrich Reck-Malleczewen](/wiki/Friedrich_Reck-Malleczewen "Friedrich Reck-Malleczewen"), an anti-Nazi German conservative, recorded in his journal that

> Swarms of Berlin League of German Maidens girls have been directed here for the moment, and they wave ecstatically at the tank columns rattling through the old streets. In the next issue of the *Berliner Illustrierte Zeitung* they will be pictured as 'Local Inhabitants, Who Greeted Their German Liberators with Wild Ovations'.... We know all about the talent for stagecraft of Goebbels....[[66]](#cite_note-70)

For the *Wehrmacht*, the invasion was the first big test of its machinery. Although the invading forces were badly organized and coordination among the units was poor, it mattered little because the Austrian government had ordered the Austrian [Bundesheer](/wiki/Austrian_Armed_Forces "Austrian Armed Forces") not to resist.[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECarr198185-71)

That afternoon, Hitler, riding in a car, crossed the border at his birthplace, [Braunau am Inn](/wiki/Braunau_am_Inn "Braunau am Inn"), with a 4,000 man bodyguard.[[61]](#cite_note-otr.com-64) In the evening, he arrived at [Linz](/wiki/Linz "Linz") and was given an enthusiastic welcome. 250,000 Austrians gathered in Linz to meet Adolf Hitler and support *Anschluss*.[[68]](#cite_note-72) The enthusiasm displayed toward Hitler and the Germans surprised both Nazis and non-Nazis, as most people had believed that a majority of Austrians opposed *Anschluss*.[[69]](#cite_note-macdonogh2009-73)[[70]](#cite_note-74) Many Germans from both Austria and Germany welcomed the *Anschluss* as they saw it as completing the complex and long overdue unification of all Germans into one state.[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStackelberg1999170-75) On 13 March Seyss-Inquart announced the abrogation of [Article 88 of the Treaty of Saint-Germain](/wiki/Treaty_of_Saint-Germain-en-Laye_(1919)#Politics_and_military "Treaty of Saint-Germain-en-Laye (1919)"), which prohibited the unification of Austria and Germany, and approved the replacement of the [Austrian states](/wiki/States_of_Austria "States of Austria") with *[Reichsgaue](/wiki/Reichsgau "Reichsgau")*.[[69]](#cite_note-macdonogh2009-73) The seizure of Austria demonstrated once again Hitler's aggressive territorial ambitions, and, once again, the failure of the British and the French to take action against him for violating the Versailles Treaty. Their lack of will emboldened him toward further aggression.[[72]](#cite_note-76)

Hitler's journey through Austria became a triumphal tour that climaxed in [Vienna](/wiki/Vienna "Vienna") on 15 March 1938, when around 200,000 cheering German Austrians gathered around the *[Heldenplatz](/wiki/Heldenplatz "Heldenplatz")* (Square of Heroes) to hear Hitler say that "The oldest eastern province of the German people shall be, from this point on, the newest bastion of the German Reich"[[73]](#cite_note-77) followed by his "greatest accomplishment" (completing the annexing of Austria to form a Greater German Reich) by saying "As leader and chancellor of the German nation and Reich I announce to German history now the entry of my homeland into the German Reich."[[74]](#cite_note-78)[[75]](#cite_note-79) Hitler later commented: "Certain foreign newspapers have said that we fell on Austria with brutal methods. I can only say: even in death they cannot stop lying. I have in the course of my political struggle won much love from my people, but when I crossed the former frontier (into Austria) there met me such a stream of love as I have never experienced. Not as tyrants have we come, but as liberators."[[76]](#cite_note-80)

Hitler said as a personal note to the *Anschluss*: "I, myself, as Führer and Chancellor, will be happy to walk on the soil of the country that is my home as a free German citizen."[[77]](#cite_note-81)[[78]](#cite_note-82)

Hitler's popularity reached an unprecedented peak after he fulfilled the *Anschluss* because he had completed the long-awaited idea of a Greater Germany. [Bismarck](/wiki/Otto_von_Bismarck "Otto von Bismarck") had not chosen to include Austria in his 1871 [unification of Germany](/wiki/Unification_of_Germany "Unification of Germany"), and there was genuine support from Germans in both Austria and Germany for an *Anschluss*.[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStackelberg1999170-75)

### Popularity of the *Anschluss*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anschluss&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Popularity of the Anschluss")]

Hitler's forces suppressed all opposition. Before the first German soldier crossed the border, [Heinrich Himmler](/wiki/Heinrich_Himmler "Heinrich Himmler") and a few [Schutzstaffel](/wiki/Schutzstaffel "Schutzstaffel") (SS) officers landed in Vienna to arrest prominent representatives of the First Republic, such as [Richard Schmitz](/wiki/Richard_Schmitz "Richard Schmitz"), [Leopold Figl](/wiki/Leopold_Figl "Leopold Figl"), [Friedrich Hillegeist](/wiki/Friedrich_Hillegeist "Friedrich Hillegeist"), and [Franz Olah](/wiki/Franz_Olah "Franz Olah"). During the few weeks between the *Anschluss* and the plebiscite, authorities rounded up Social Democrats, Communists, other potential political dissenters, and [Austrian Jews](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Austria "History of the Jews in Austria"), and imprisoned them or sent them to [concentration camps](/wiki/Nazi_concentration_camps "Nazi concentration camps"). Within a few days of 12 March, 70,000 people had been arrested. The disused northwest railway station in Vienna was converted into a makeshift concentration camp.[[79]](#cite_note-83) American historian Evan Burr Bukey warned that the plebiscite result needs to be taken with "great caution".[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBukey200038-84) The plebiscite was subject to large-scale Nazi propaganda and to the abrogation of the voting rights of around 360,000 people (8% of the eligible voting population), mainly political enemies such as former members of left-wing parties and Austrian citizens of Jewish or Romani origin.[[81]](#cite_note-85)[[82]](#cite_note-86)[[83]](#cite_note-Austrian_Resistance-87)[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBukey200038-84)

The Austrians' support for the *Anschluss* was ambivalent; the Austrian population was given no choice and was subjected to extensive intimidation and suppression of the political opposition, as at the time of the plebiscite, the annexation of Austria was a 'fait accompli' as the German army had already occupied Austria and integrated it into Germany.[[84]](#cite_note-88) American historian Evan Bukey argues that there was a genuine German nationalist feeling in Austria amongst at least a part of the population, and those holding antisemitic sentiments were more than ready to "fulfill their duty" in the "Greater German Reich".[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBukey200038–39-89) Bukey also states that since the [Social Democratic Party of Austria](/wiki/Social_Democratic_Party_of_Austria "Social Democratic Party of Austria") leader [Karl Renner](/wiki/Karl_Renner "Karl Renner") and the highest representative of the Roman Catholic church in Austria Cardinal [Theodor Innitzer](/wiki/Theodor_Innitzer "Theodor Innitzer") both endorsed the *Anschluss*, approximately two-thirds of Austrians might have voted for it.[[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBukey200038-84)

Similary, Austrian historian Gerhard Botz claimed that the Nazis did not falsify the results of the referendum and assumed that the "yes"-votes were in the range of 90 to 99 percent. In addition to the takeover of power that had already taken place and the intimidation of the population through terror, Botz contends that Cardinal Innitzer's and Karl Renner's endorsements opened the National Socialists inroads to the Catholic-conservative and socialist camps, which together comprised two-thirds of the population. Moreover, Botz argues that unemployed Austrians supported Anschluss because they benefited from the German unemployment insurance program, which offered them positions left vacant the expulsion of Austrian Jews; he also notes that the National Socialist propaganda emphasized that unemployment was all but eliminated in the Germany and appealed to nationalist sentiments while building up an internal enemy through anti-Semitism.[[86]](#cite_note-90)

On the contrary, Julie Thorpe notes that endorsements of people such as Renner "do not stand on their own as evidence for broad pan-German sympathies amongst Austria’s working population".[[87]](#cite_note-91) British historian [Donny Gluckstein](/wiki/Donny_Gluckstein "Donny Gluckstein") notes that Austrian socialists reacted with "disgust" to Renner's endorsement of Anschluss, provoking a split in the [SPÖ](/wiki/SP%C3%96 "SPÖ"). Austrian left circles vehemently opposed Anschluss, and Renner's declaration prompted many to defect to Revolutionary Socialists under [Otto Bauer](/wiki/Otto_Bauer "Otto Bauer") or the [KPÖ](/wiki/KP%C3%96 "KPÖ").[[88]](#cite_note-gluckstein_137-92) The relevance of Innitzer's endorsement is also disputed – he was reportedly "despised" by Austrian workers,[[89]](#cite_note-93) and the Anschluss sparked Catholic protests in Austria under the slogan "Our Führer is Christ" (rather than Hitler).[[88]](#cite_note-gluckstein_137-92)

According to the Austrian historian Alfred D. Low, one of the reasons why Germany did not allow the plebiscite to be held by the Austrian government was that the Nazi regime feared to be defeated at the polls; Low states that in 1938, there was "majority support to Austria's independence".[[90]](#cite_note-94) Karina V. Korostelina also notes the oppression factor, writing that the Nazis "immediately began the execution of Jews as well as those Austrians who openly opposed the Anschluss."[[91]](#cite_note-95) Political scientist [Eric Voegelin](/wiki/Eric_Voegelin "Eric Voegelin"), who fled Austria shortly after Anschluss, wrote that "there was not much doubt that in 1938 a majority of Austrians did not favor a union with Germany."[[92]](#cite_note-96)

According to Hungarian historian [Oszkár Jászi](/wiki/Oszk%C3%A1r_J%C3%A1szi "Oszkár Jászi"), writing in 1938, the idea of *Anschluss* was opposed amongst most political circles in Austria. Jászi noted that "the annihilation of the German labor movement showed to Austrian socialism what it could expect from an Anschluss under Nazi rule", while "Austrian Catholicism realized what its fate would be under a system which crushed the great Catholic Party of Germany, [the Centrum](/wiki/Catholic_Center_Party "Catholic Center Party")".[[93]](#cite_note-jaszi-97) It was also opposed by other groups, such as the Austrian Jews as well as "old Hapsburgist officers and officials and by a considerable part of Austrian capitalism". Most contemporary writers estimated that about two-thirds of Austrians wanted Austria to remain independent.[[93]](#cite_note-jaszi-97)

How many Austrians behind closed doors were against the *Anschluss* remains unknown, but only one "unhappy face" of an Austrian in public when the Germans marched into Austria has ever been produced.[[94]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBukey200033-98) According to some [Gestapo](/wiki/Gestapo "Gestapo") reports, only a quarter to a third of Austrian voters in Vienna were in favour of the *Anschluss*.[[95]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEvans2006655-99) In most rural areas, especially in Tyrol, the support for the Anschluss was even lower.[[96]](#cite_note-Encarta-100) According to Evan Burr Bukey, no more than one-third of Austrians ever fully supported Nazism during the existence of Nazi Germany.[[97]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBukey200033–34-101) According to the estimates of the Austrian government, with the [voting age](/wiki/Voting_age "Voting age") of 24, about 70% of Austrians would have voted to preserve the Austrian independence.[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKnaur1951367–369-59) Czech-American historian [Radomír Luža](https://cs.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radom%C3%ADr_Lu%C5%BEa "cz:Radomír Luža") estimated that between 65% and 75% of Austrians supported the continuation of Austrian independence.[[98]](#cite_note-luza_52-102) About a quarter of the Austrian population was estimated to be supportive of the [NSDAP](/wiki/NSDAP "NSDAP").[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKnaur1951367–369-59)

The newly installed Nazis, within two days, transferred power to Germany, and *Wehrmacht* troops entered Austria to enforce the *Anschluss*. The Nazis held a controlled plebiscite (*Volksabstimmung*) in the whole Reich within the following month, asking the people to ratify the *fait accompli*, and claimed that 99.7561% of the votes cast in Austria were in favor.[[99]](#cite_note-103)[[100]](#cite_note-104)

Although the [Allies](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_I "Allies of World War I") were committed to upholding the terms of the [Treaty of Versailles](/wiki/Treaty_of_Versailles "Treaty of Versailles") and those of [St. Germain](/wiki/Treaty_of_Saint-Germain-en-Laye_(1919) "Treaty of Saint-Germain-en-Laye (1919)"), which specifically prohibited the union of Austria and Germany, their reaction was only verbal and moderate. No military confrontation took place, and even the strongest voices against the annexation, particularly, [France](/wiki/Third_French_Republic "Third French Republic"), and Great Britain remained at peace. The loudest verbal protest was voiced by the government of [Mexico](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico").[[101]](#cite_note-105)

Germany, which had a shortage of steel and a weak [balance of payments](/wiki/Balance_of_payments "Balance of payments"), gained iron ore mines in the [Erzberg](/wiki/Erzberg "Erzberg") and 748 million [RM](/wiki/Reichsmark "Reichsmark") in the reserves of Austria's central bank [Oesterreichische Nationalbank](/wiki/Oesterreichische_Nationalbank "Oesterreichische Nationalbank"), more than twice its own cash.[[69]](#cite_note-macdonogh2009-73) In the years that followed, some bank accounts were transferred from Austria to Germany as "enemy property accounts".[[102]](#cite_note-106)

### Persecution of the Jews

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anschluss&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Persecution of the Jews")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7b/Austrian_Nazis_and_local_residents_watch_as_Jews_are_forced_to_scrub_the_pavement_after_Nazi_annexation.jpg/330px-Austrian_Nazis_and_local_residents_watch_as_Jews_are_forced_to_scrub_the_pavement_after_Nazi_annexation.jpg)](/wiki/File:Austrian_Nazis_and_local_residents_watch_as_Jews_are_forced_to_scrub_the_pavement_after_Nazi_annexation.jpg)

Immediately after the *Anschluss*, Vienna's Jews were forced to wash pro-independence slogans (*[Reibpartie](/w/index.php?title=Reibpartie&action=edit&redlink=1 "Reibpartie (page does not exist)") [[de](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Reibpartie "de:Reibpartie")]*) from the city's pavements.

The campaign against the Jews began immediately after the *Anschluss*. They were driven through the streets of Vienna, their homes and shops were plundered. Jewish men and women were forced to wash away pro-independence slogans painted on the streets of Vienna ahead of the failed 13 March plebiscite.[[103]](#cite_note-Snyder2015-107)[[104]](#cite_note-108) Jewish actresses from the [Theater in der Josefstadt](/wiki/Theater_in_der_Josefstadt "Theater in der Josefstadt") were forced to clean toilets by the [SA](/wiki/Sturmabteilung "Sturmabteilung"). The process of [Aryanisation](/wiki/Aryanization_(Nazism) "Aryanization (Nazism)") began, and Jews were driven out of public life within months.[[105]](#cite_note-KohlRobertson2006-109) These events reached a climax in the [Kristallnacht](/wiki/Kristallnacht "Kristallnacht") [pogrom](/wiki/Pogrom "Pogrom") of 9–10 November 1938. All synagogues and prayer houses in Vienna were destroyed, as well as in other Austrian cities such as Salzburg. The [Stadttempel](/wiki/Stadttempel "Stadttempel") was the sole survivor due to its location in a residential district which prevented it from being burned down. Most Jewish shops were plundered and closed. Over 6,000 Jews were arrested overnight, the majority deported to [Dachau concentration camp](/wiki/Dachau_concentration_camp "Dachau concentration camp") in the following days.[[106]](#cite_note-McKale2006-110) The [Nuremberg Laws](/wiki/Nuremberg_Laws "Nuremberg Laws") applied in Austria from May 1938, later reinforced with innumerable antisemitic decrees. Jews were gradually robbed of their freedoms, blocked from almost all professions, shut out of schools and universities, and forced to wear the [yellow badge](/wiki/Yellow_badge "Yellow badge") from September 1941.[[107]](#cite_note-Wistrich1992-111)

The Nazis dissolved Jewish organisations and institutions, hoping to force Jews to emigrate. Their plans succeeded—by the end of 1941, 130,000 Jews had left Vienna, 30,000 of whom went to the United States. They left behind all of their property, but were forced to pay the [Reich Flight Tax](/wiki/Reich_Flight_Tax "Reich Flight Tax"), a tax on all émigrés from Nazi Germany; some received financial support from international aid organisations so that they could pay this tax. The majority of the Jews who had stayed in Vienna eventually became victims of the [Holocaust](/wiki/Holocaust "Holocaust"). Of the more than 65,000 Viennese Jews who were deported to concentration camps, fewer than 2,000 survived.[[108]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPauley2000297–298-112)

## Referendum

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anschluss&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Referendum")]

Main article: [1938 Austrian Anschluss referendum](/wiki/1938_Austrian_Anschluss_referendum "1938 Austrian Anschluss referendum")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/Stimmzettel-Anschluss.jpg/330px-Stimmzettel-Anschluss.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stimmzettel-Anschluss.jpg)

Voting ballot from 10 April 1938. The ballot text reads "Do you agree with the reunification of Austria with the German Reich that was enacted on 13 March 1938, and do you vote for the party of our leader Adolf Hitler?" The large circle is labelled "Yes", the smaller "No".

The *Anschluss* was given immediate effect by legislative act on 13 March, subject to ratification by a [referendum](/wiki/Referendum "Referendum"). Austria became the province of [Ostmark](/wiki/Ostmark_(Austria) "Ostmark (Austria)"), and Seyss-Inquart was appointed governor. The [referendum](/wiki/1938_Austrian_Anschluss_referendum "1938 Austrian Anschluss referendum") was held on 10 April and officially recorded a support of 99.7% of the voters.[[83]](#cite_note-Austrian_Resistance-87)

While historians concur that the votes were accurately counted, the process was neither free nor secret. Officials were present directly beside the voting booths and received the voting ballot by hand (in contrast to a [secret vote](/wiki/Secret_ballot "Secret ballot") where the voting ballot is inserted into a closed box). In the referendum on 10 April, 73.3% of votes in [Innervillgraten](/wiki/Innervillgraten "Innervillgraten") were in favor of the *Anschluss*, which was the lowest number of all Austrian municipalities.[[109]](#cite_note-Tirol_multimedial-113)

Austria remained part of Germany until the end of World War II. A provisional government in [Allied-occupied Austria](/wiki/Allied-occupied_Austria "Allied-occupied Austria") declared the *Anschluss* "null und nichtig" ([null and void](/wiki/Null_and_void "Null and void")) on 27 April 1945.[[110]](#cite_note-114) Henceforth, Austria was recognized as a separate country, although it remained divided into [occupation zones](/wiki/Allied-occupied_Austria "Allied-occupied Austria") and controlled by the [Allied Commission](/wiki/Allied_Commission#Austria "Allied Commission") until 1955, when the [Austrian State Treaty](/wiki/Austrian_State_Treaty "Austrian State Treaty") restored its sovereignty.

## Reactions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anschluss&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Reactions")]

Austria in the first days of Nazi Germany's control had many contradictions: at one and the same time, Hitler's regime began to tighten its grip on every aspect of society, beginning with mass arrests as thousands of Austrians tried to escape; yet other Austrians cheered and welcomed the German troops entering their territory.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/11/Bundesarchiv_Bild_192-334%2C_KZ_Mauthausen%2C_Garagenhofeinfahrt.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_192-334%2C_KZ_Mauthausen%2C_Garagenhofeinfahrt.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_192-334,_KZ_Mauthausen,_Garagenhofeinfahrt.jpg)

Gate to the garage yard in the [Mauthausen-Gusen](/wiki/Mauthausen-Gusen "Mauthausen-Gusen") concentration camp

In March 1938 the local Gauleiter of [Gmunden](/wiki/Gmunden "Gmunden"), [Upper Austria](/wiki/Upper_Austria "Upper Austria"), gave a speech to the local Austrians and told them in plain terms that all "traitors" of Austria were to be thrown into the newly opened concentration camp at [Mauthausen-Gusen](/wiki/Mauthausen-Gusen "Mauthausen-Gusen").[[111]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGellately200269-115) The camp became notorious for its cruelty and barbarism. During its existence an estimated 200,000 people died, half of whom were directly killed.[[111]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGellately200269-115)

The [antigypsy](/wiki/Anti-Romanyism "Anti-Romanyism") sentiment was implemented initially most harshly in Austria when between 1938 and 1939 the Nazis arrested around 2,000 Romani men who were sent to [Dachau](/wiki/Dachau_concentration_camp "Dachau concentration camp") and 1,000 Romani women who were sent to [Ravensbrück](/wiki/Ravensbr%C3%BCck_concentration_camp "Ravensbrück concentration camp").[[112]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGellately2002108-116) Starting in 1939, Austrian Romani had to register themselves to local authorities.[[113]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGellately2001222-117) The Nazis began to publish articles linking the Romani with criminality.[[113]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGellately2001222-117) Until 1942, the Nazis had made a distinction between "pure Gypsies" and "Gypsy *[Mischlinges](/wiki/Mischlinge "Mischlinge")*" ("mixlings" or "half-breeds").[[114]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGellately2001225-118) However, Nazi racial research claimed that 90% of Romani were of mixed ancestry. Subsequently, the Nazis ordered that the Romani were to be treated on the same level as the Jews.[[114]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGellately2001225-118)

After breaking off the negotiations regarding the position of the Catholic Church in Austria, Cardinal [Theodor Innitzer](/wiki/Theodor_Innitzer "Theodor Innitzer") (a political figure of the CS) was intimidated into supporting the Anschluss after being assaulted.[[115]](#cite_note-krieger-119) [Vatican Radio](/wiki/Vatican_Radio "Vatican Radio"), however, broadcast a strong denunciation of the German action, and [Cardinal Eugenio Pacelli](/wiki/Pope_Pius_XII "Pope Pius XII"), the [Vatican Secretary of State](/wiki/Cardinal_Secretary_of_State "Cardinal Secretary of State"), ordered Innitzer to report to Rome. Before meeting the Pope, Innitzer met Pacelli, who had been outraged by Innitzer's statement. He told Innitzer to retract his statement; he was made to sign a new statement, issued on behalf of all the Austrian bishops, that stated: "The solemn declaration of the Austrian bishops... was clearly not intended to be an approval of something that was not and is not compatible with God's law".[[116]](#cite_note-120) Describing the Church's reaction to Anschluss, historian [Ronald J. Rychlak](/wiki/Ronald_J._Rychlak "Ronald J. Rychlak") wrote:

> Vatican Radio immediately broadcast a vehement denunciation of the Anschluss and the reaction of the Austrian Catholic Church. [...] On October 21, in one of his last public appearances, [Pius](/wiki/Pope_Pius_XI "Pope Pius XI") personally attacked Hitler, likening him to [Julian the Apostate](/wiki/Julian_the_Apostate "Julian the Apostate") (Roman Emperor Flavius Claudius Julianus), who attempted to “saddle the Christians with responsibility for the persecution he had unleashed against them.”[[117]](#cite_note-121)

The Vatican newspaper reported that the German bishops' earlier statement had been issued without approval from Rome. The Vatican condemned Nazism in its newspaper *[L'Osservatore Romano](/wiki/L%27Osservatore_Romano "L'Osservatore Romano")*, and forbade Catholics from following their ideas or supporting Anschluss.[[118]](#cite_note-catholic_church-122) On 11 March 1938, one day before the occupation of Austria by the Wehrmacht, the [Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Vienna](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_Vienna "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Vienna") issued an appeal to Austrians: "As Austrian citizens, we stand and we fight for a free and independent Austria".[[119]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrook-Shepherd1963202"Exactly_one_week_before,_on_11_March,_the_same_Archdiocese_of_Vienna_had_issued_the_following_glowing_appeal_in_support_of_Schuschnigg's_abortive_anti-Hitler_poll:_'As_Austrian_citizens,_we_stand_and_we_fight_for_a_free_and_independent_Austria!"-123)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/Bundesarchiv_Bild_192-269%2C_KZ_Mauthausen%2C_H%C3%A4ftlinge_im_Steinbruch.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_192-269%2C_KZ_Mauthausen%2C_H%C3%A4ftlinge_im_Steinbruch.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_192-269,_KZ_Mauthausen,_H%C3%A4ftlinge_im_Steinbruch.jpg)

"Stairs of Death" at [Mauthausen-Gusen](/wiki/Mauthausen-Gusen "Mauthausen-Gusen") with prisoners forced to carry a granite block up 186 steps to the top of the quarry

Robert Kauer, president of the minority [Lutheran Church](/wiki/Lutheran_Church "Lutheran Church") in Austria, greeted Hitler on 13 March as "saviour of the 350,000 German Protestants in Austria and liberator from a five-year hardship".[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] [Karl Renner](/wiki/Karl_Renner "Karl Renner"), the most famous Social Democrat of the First Republic, announced his support for the *Anschluss* and appealed to all Austrians to vote in favour of it on 10 April.[[96]](#cite_note-Encarta-100)

The international response to the *Anschluss* was publicly moderate. *[The Times](/wiki/The_Times "The Times")* commented that 300 years before, Scotland had [joined](/wiki/Acts_of_Union_1707 "Acts of Union 1707") England as well and that this event would not really differ much. On 14 March, the British Prime Minister, [Neville Chamberlain](/wiki/Neville_Chamberlain "Neville Chamberlain") spoke about the "Austrian situation" in the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "House of Commons of the United Kingdom"). He noted that the British ambassador in Berlin objected to the use of "coercion, backed by force" that would undermine Austria's independence.[[120]](#cite_note-124) Within this speech Chamberlain also said, "The hard fact is that nothing could have arrested what has actually happened [in Austria] unless this country and other countries had been prepared to use force."[[121]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShirer1984308-125) Chamberlain informed the [Foreign Policy Committee](/wiki/Foreign_Affairs_Select_Committee "Foreign Affairs Select Committee") that the Anschluss would not alter the [National Government](/wiki/National_Government_(1937%E2%80%931939) "National Government (1937–1939)")'s [European policy](/wiki/European_foreign_policy_of_the_Chamberlain_ministry "European foreign policy of the Chamberlain ministry").[[122]](#cite_note-126)

On 18 March 1938, the German government communicated to the Secretary General of the [League of Nations](/wiki/League_of_Nations "League of Nations") about the inclusion of Austria.[[123]](#cite_note-127) And the next day in Geneva, the Mexican Delegate to the International Office of Labor, [Isidro Fabela](/wiki/Isidro_Fabela "Isidro Fabela"), voiced an energetic protest, stronger than that expressed by European countries,[[124]](#cite_note-128) denouncing the annexation of Austria by Nazi Germany.[[125]](#cite_note-129)[[126]](#cite_note-130)

## Legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anschluss&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Legacy")]

### Meaning of *Anschluss*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anschluss&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Meaning of Anschluss")]

The word *Anschluss* is properly translated as "joinder", "connection", "unification", or "political union". In contrast, the German word *Annektierung* (military annexation) was not used, and is not commonly used now, to describe the union of Austria and Germany in 1938. The word *Anschluss* had been widespread before 1938 describing an incorporation of Austria into Germany. Calling the incorporation of Austria into Germany an "Anschluss," that is a "unification" or "joinder", was also part of the propaganda used in 1938 by Nazi Germany to create the impression that the union was not coerced. Hitler described the incorporation of Austria as a *Heimkehr*, a return to its original home.[[127]](#cite_note-131) The word *Anschluss* has endured since 1938.

Some sources, like the *[Encyclopædia Britannica](/wiki/Encyclop%C3%A6dia_Britannica "Encyclopædia Britannica")*, describe the *Anschluss* as an "annexation"[[128]](#cite_note-132) rather than a union.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9e/Nazi_Germany.svg/500px-Nazi_Germany.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Nazi_Germany.svg)

A map showing the border changes of Germany in the various years 1933 (red), 1939 (pink) and 1943 (orange)

### Changes in Central Europe

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anschluss&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Changes in Central Europe")]

The *Anschluss* was among the first major steps in Austrian-born Hitler's desire to create a [Greater German Reich](/wiki/German_question#Later_influence "German question") that was to include all [ethnic Germans](/wiki/Ethnic_Germans "Ethnic Germans") and all the [lands and territories](/wiki/Treaty_of_Versailles#Territorial_changes "Treaty of Versailles") that the [German Empire](/wiki/German_Empire "German Empire") had lost after the [First World War](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I").
