Although Austria was predominantly ethnically German and had been part of the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire") until it dissolved in 1806 and the [German Confederation](/wiki/German_Confederation "German Confederation")[[129]](#cite_note-Heeren1873_p480-482-133) until 1866 after the defeat in the [Austro-Prussian War](/wiki/Austro-Prussian_War "Austro-Prussian War"), it had never been a part of the German Empire. The [unification of Germany](/wiki/Unification_of_Germany "Unification of Germany") brought about by [Otto von Bismarck](/wiki/Otto_von_Bismarck "Otto von Bismarck") created that [Prussian](/wiki/Prussia "Prussia")-dominated entity in 1871, with Austria, Prussia's rival for dominance of the German states, explicitly excluded.[[130]](#cite_note-134)

Prior to annexing Austria in 1938, Nazi Germany had [remilitarized](/wiki/Remilitarization_of_the_Rhineland "Remilitarization of the Rhineland") the [Rhineland](/wiki/Rhineland "Rhineland"), and the [Saar](/wiki/Saarland#History "Saarland") region was returned to Germany after 15 years of occupation through a plebiscite. After the *Anschluss*, Hitler targeted Czechoslovakia, provoking an international crisis which led to the [Munich Agreement](/wiki/Munich_Agreement "Munich Agreement") in September 1938, giving Nazi Germany control of the industrial [Sudetenland](/wiki/Sudetenland "Sudetenland"), which had a predominantly ethnic German population. In March 1939, Hitler then dismantled Czechoslovakia by recognising the independence of [Slovakia](/wiki/Slovakia "Slovakia") and making the rest of the nation a [protectorate](/wiki/Protectorate "Protectorate"). That same year, [Memelland](/wiki/Memelland "Memelland") was returned from Lithuania.

With the *Anschluss*, the Republic of Austria ceased to exist as an independent state. At the end of World War II, a Provisional Austrian Government under [Karl Renner](/wiki/Karl_Renner "Karl Renner") was set up by conservatives, Social Democrats and Communists on 27 April 1945 (when Vienna had already been occupied by the [Red Army](/wiki/Red_Army "Red Army")). It cancelled the *Anschluss* the same day and was legally recognized by the [Allies](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_II "Allies of World War II") in the following months. In 1955 the [Austrian State Treaty](/wiki/Austrian_State_Treaty "Austrian State Treaty") re-established Austria as a sovereign state.

### Second Republic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anschluss&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Second Republic")]

#### Moscow Declaration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anschluss&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Moscow Declaration")]

The [Moscow Declaration](/wiki/Moscow_Declaration "Moscow Declaration") of 1943, signed by the United States, the Soviet Union and the United Kingdom, included a "Declaration on Austria", which stated:

> The governments of the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"), the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") and the [United States of America](/wiki/United_States "United States") are agreed that Austria, the first free country to fall a victim to Hitlerite aggression, shall be liberated from German domination.
>
> They regard the annexation imposed on Austria by Germany on 15 March 1938, as null and void. They consider themselves as in no way bound by any changes effected in Austria since that date. They declare that they wish to see re-established a free and independent Austria and thereby to open the way for the Austrian people themselves, as well as those neighbouring States which will be faced with similar problems, to find that political and economic security which is the only basis for lasting peace.
>
> Austria is reminded, however, that she has a responsibility, which she cannot evade, for participation in the war at the side of Hitlerite Germany, and that in the final settlement account will inevitably be taken of her own contribution to her liberation.[[131]](#cite_note-135)[[132]](#cite_note-136)

The Moscow Declaration is said to have a somewhat complex drafting history.[[133]](#cite_note-137) The declaration was mostly intended to serve as propaganda aimed at stirring [Austrian resistance](/wiki/Austrian_resistance "Austrian resistance"). Although some Austrians aided [Jews](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Austria "History of the Jews in Austria") and are counted as [Righteous Among the Nations](/wiki/Righteous_Among_the_Nations "Righteous Among the Nations"), there never was an effective Austrian armed resistance of the sort found in other [countries under German occupation](/wiki/German-occupied_Europe "German-occupied Europe").

Despite the declaration, the [Nuremberg trials](/wiki/Nuremberg_trials "Nuremberg trials") decided against classifying participation in planning of the Anschluss as a crime against peace, stating in the judgement of [Ernst Kaltenbrunner](/wiki/Ernst_Kaltenbrunner "Ernst Kaltenbrunner") (who was nonetheless convicted and sentenced to death for other war crimes) that "the Anschluss, although it was an aggressive act, is not charged as an aggressive war."[[134]](#cite_note-138) In most cases this distinction proved a moot point, as the Nazis responsible for planning the Anschluss were either dead by the time of the Nuremberg trials, or were convicted for their roles in planning the invasions of other countries. [Arthur Seyss-Inquart](/wiki/Arthur_Seyss-Inquart "Arthur Seyss-Inquart")[[135]](#cite_note-139) and [Franz von Papen](/wiki/Franz_von_Papen "Franz von Papen"),[[136]](#cite_note-140) in particular, were both indicted under count one (conspiracy to commit crimes against peace) specifically for their activities in support of the Austrian Nazi Party and the *Anschluss*, but neither was convicted of this count. In acquitting von Papen, the court noted that his actions were in its view political immoralities but not crimes under its charter. Seyss-Inquart was convicted of other serious war crimes, most of which took place in Poland and the Netherlands, was sentenced to death and executed.

#### Austrian identity and the "victim theory"

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anschluss&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Austrian identity and the \"victim theory\"")]

Main article: [Austria – the Nazis' first victim](/wiki/Austria_%E2%80%93_the_Nazis%27_first_victim "Austria – the Nazis' first victim")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/05/Rot-Weiss-Rot-Buch_1946.jpg/250px-Rot-Weiss-Rot-Buch_1946.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rot-Weiss-Rot-Buch_1946.jpg)

"Red-White-Red Book" published by the Austrian Ministry of Foreign Affairs in 1946 describes the events of Austria between 1938 and 1945 by the Founders of the Second Austrian Republic.

From 1949 to 1988, many [Austrians](/wiki/Austrians "Austrians") sought comfort in the idea of Austria as being the [first victim of the Nazis](/wiki/Austria_%E2%80%94_the_Nazis%27_first_victim "Austria — the Nazis' first victim") (*Opferthese*). Although the Nazi party was promptly banned, Austria did not have the same thorough process of [denazification](/wiki/Denazification "Denazification") that was imposed on Germany. Lacking outside pressure for political reform, factions of Austrian society tried for a long time to advance the view that the *Anschluss* was "only an annexation at the point of a bayonet".[[137]](#cite_note-141)

This view of the events of 1938 has deep roots in the 10 years of Allied occupation and the struggle to regain Austrian sovereignty: the "[victim theory](/wiki/Victim_theory "Victim theory")" played an essential role in the negotiations for the [Austrian State Treaty](/wiki/Austrian_State_Treaty "Austrian State Treaty") with the Soviets, and by pointing to the Moscow Declaration, Austrian politicians heavily relied on it to achieve a solution for Austria different from the division of Germany into separate Eastern and Western states. The state treaty, alongside the subsequent Austrian declaration of permanent [neutrality](/wiki/Neutral_country "Neutral country"), marked important milestones for the solidification of Austria's independent national identity during the course of the following decades.[[138]](#cite_note-142)

As Austrian politicians of the left and right attempted to reconcile their differences to avoid the violent conflict that had dominated the First Republic, discussions of both [Austrian Nazism](/wiki/Austrian_Nazism "Austrian Nazism") and Austria's role during the Nazi-era were largely avoided. Still, the [Austrian People's Party](/wiki/Austrian_People%27s_Party "Austrian People's Party") (ÖVP) had advanced, and still advances, the argument that the establishment of the Dollfuss dictatorship was necessary to maintain Austrian independence. On the other hand, the [Austrian Social Democratic Party](/wiki/Austrian_Social_Democratic_Party "Austrian Social Democratic Party") (SPÖ) argues that the Dollfuss dictatorship stripped the country of the democratic resources necessary to repel Hitler; yet it ignores the fact that Hitler himself was a native of Austria.[[139]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEArt2006101-143)

It has also helped the Austrians develop their own [national identity](/wiki/National_identity "National identity") as before. After [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") and the fall of Nazi Germany the political ideology of [Pan-Germanism](/wiki/Pan-Germanism "Pan-Germanism") fell into disfavor and is now seen by the majority of German-speaking people as taboo.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Unlike earlier in the 20th century when there was no Austrian identity separate from a German one, in 1987 only 6% of the Austrians identified themselves as "Germans."[[140]](#cite_note-144) A survey carried out in 2008 found that 82% of Austrians considered themselves to be their own nation.[[141]](#cite_note-145)

#### Political events

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anschluss&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Political events")]

For decades, the victim theory remained largely undisputed in Austria. The public was rarely forced to confront the legacy of Nazi Germany. One of those occasions arose in 1965, when [Taras Borodajkewycz](/wiki/Taras_Borodajkewycz "Taras Borodajkewycz"), a professor of economic history, made antisemitic remarks following the death of [Ernst Kirchweger](/wiki/Ernst_Kirchweger "Ernst Kirchweger"), a concentration camp survivor killed by a right-wing protester during riots. It was not until the 1980s that Austrians confronted their mixed past on a large scale. The catalyst for the *[Vergangenheitsbewältigung](/wiki/Vergangenheitsbew%C3%A4ltigung "Vergangenheitsbewältigung")* (struggle to come to terms with the past) was the Waldheim affair. [Kurt Waldheim](/wiki/Kurt_Waldheim "Kurt Waldheim"), a successful candidate in the [1986 Austrian presidential election](/wiki/1986_Austrian_presidential_election "1986 Austrian presidential election") and former [UN Secretary-General](/wiki/Secretary-General_of_the_United_Nations "Secretary-General of the United Nations"), was accused of having been a member of the Nazi party and of the [Sturmabteilung](/wiki/Sturmabteilung "Sturmabteilung") (SA). He was later absolved of direct involvement in [war crimes](/wiki/War_crimes "War crimes"). The Waldheim affair started the first serious discussions about Austria's past and the *Anschluss*.

Another factor was the rise of [Jörg Haider](/wiki/J%C3%B6rg_Haider "Jörg Haider") and the [Freedom Party of Austria](/wiki/Freedom_Party_of_Austria "Freedom Party of Austria") (FPÖ) in the 1980s. The party had combined elements of the [pan-German](/wiki/Pan-German "Pan-German") right with [free-market liberalism](/wiki/Free-market_liberalism "Free-market liberalism") since its foundation in 1955, but after Haider ascended to the party chairmanship in 1986, the liberal elements became increasingly marginalized. Haider began to openly use nationalist and anti-immigrant rhetoric. He was criticised for using the *völkisch* (ethnic) definition of national interest ("Austria for Austrians") and his apologetics for Austria's past, notably calling members of the [Waffen-SS](/wiki/Waffen-SS "Waffen-SS") "men of honour". Following a dramatic rise in electoral support in the 1990s that peaked in the [1999 elections](/wiki/1999_Austria_legislative_election "1999 Austria legislative election"), the FPÖ entered a coalition with the [Austrian People's Party](/wiki/Austrian_People%27s_Party "Austrian People's Party") (ÖVP), led by [Wolfgang Schüssel](/wiki/Wolfgang_Sch%C3%BCssel "Wolfgang Schüssel"). This was condemned in 2000. The coalition prompted the regular *Donnerstagsdemonstrationen* (Thursday demonstrations) in protest against the government, which took place on the [Heldenplatz](/wiki/Heldenplatz "Heldenplatz") where Hitler had greeted the masses during the *Anschluss*. Haider's tactics and rhetoric, often criticised as sympathetic to Nazism, forced Austrians to reconsider their relationship to the past. Haider's coalition partner, former Chancellor [Wolfgang Schüssel](/wiki/Wolfgang_Sch%C3%BCssel "Wolfgang Schüssel"), in a 2000 interview with *[The Jerusalem Post](/wiki/The_Jerusalem_Post "The Jerusalem Post")*, reiterated the "first victim" theory.[[142]](#cite_note-146)

#### Literature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anschluss&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Literature")]

The political discussions and soul-searching were reflected in other aspects of culture. [Thomas Bernhard](/wiki/Thomas_Bernhard "Thomas Bernhard")'s last play, [*Heldenplatz*](/wiki/Heldenplatz_(play) "Heldenplatz (play)") (1988), generated controversy even before it was produced, fifty years after Hitler's entrance to the city. Bernhard made the historic elimination of references to Hitler's reception in Vienna emblematic of Austrian attempts to claim its history and culture under questionable criteria. Many politicians called Bernhard a *Nestbeschmutzer* (damaging the reputation of his country) and openly demanded that the play should not be staged in Vienna's [Burgtheater](/wiki/Burgtheater "Burgtheater"). Waldheim, still president, called the play "a crude insult to the Austrian people".[[143]](#cite_note-147)

### Historical Commission and outstanding legal issues

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anschluss&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Historical Commission and outstanding legal issues")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/Bundesarchiv_Bild_152-65-04%2C_Wien%2C_SS-Razzia_bei_j%C3%BCdischer_Gemeinde.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_Bild_152-65-04%2C_Wien%2C_SS-Razzia_bei_j%C3%BCdischer_Gemeinde.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_Bild_152-65-04,_Wien,_SS-Razzia_bei_j%C3%BCdischer_Gemeinde.jpg)

The SS raid a Jewish community center, Vienna, March 1938.

In the [Federal Republic of Germany](/wiki/Federal_Republic_of_Germany "Federal Republic of Germany") the *[Vergangenheitsbewältigung](/wiki/Vergangenheitsbew%C3%A4ltigung "Vergangenheitsbewältigung")* ("struggle to come to terms with the past") has been partially institutionalised in literary, cultural, political, and educational contexts. Austria formed a *Historikerkommission*[[144]](#cite_note-148) ("Historian's Commission" or "Historical Commission") in 1998 with a mandate to review Austria's role in the [Nazi expropriation of Jewish property](/wiki/Nazi_plunder "Nazi plunder") from a scholarly rather than legal perspective, partly in response to continuing criticism of its handling of property claims. Its membership was based on recommendations from various quarters, including [Simon Wiesenthal](/wiki/Simon_Wiesenthal "Simon Wiesenthal") and [Yad Vashem](/wiki/Yad_Vashem "Yad Vashem"). The Commission delivered its report in 2003.[[145]](#cite_note-149) Noted Holocaust historian [Raul Hilberg](/wiki/Raul_Hilberg "Raul Hilberg") refused to participate in the Commission and in an interview he stated his strenuous objections in terms both personal and in reference to larger questions about Austrian culpability and liability, comparing what he thought to be relative inattention by the [World Jewish Congress](/wiki/World_Jewish_Congress "World Jewish Congress") to the settlement governing the Swiss bank holdings of those who died or were displaced by the Holocaust.[[146]](#cite_note-150)

The [Simon Wiesenthal Center](/wiki/Simon_Wiesenthal_Center "Simon Wiesenthal Center") continues to criticise Austria (as recently as June 2005) for its alleged historical and ongoing unwillingness to aggressively pursue investigations and trials against Nazis for war crimes and crimes against humanity from the 1970s onwards. Its 2001 report offered the following characterization:

> Given the extensive participation of numerous Austrians, including at the highest levels, in the implementation of the Final Solution and other Nazi crimes, Austria should have been a leader in the prosecution of Holocaust perpetrators over the course of the past four decades, as has been the case in Germany. Unfortunately relatively little has been achieved by the Austrian authorities in this regard and in fact, with the exception of the case of [Dr. Heinrich Gross](/wiki/Heinrich_Gross "Heinrich Gross") which was suspended this year under highly suspicious circumstances (he claimed to be medically unfit, but outside the court proved to be healthy) not a single Nazi war crimes prosecution has been conducted in Austria since the mid-1970s.[[147]](#cite_note-151)

In 2003, the Center launched a worldwide effort named "Operation: Last Chance" in order to collect further information about those Nazis still alive that are potentially subject to prosecution. Although reports issued shortly thereafter credited Austria for initiating large-scale investigations, there has been one case where criticism of Austrian authorities arose recently: The Center put 92-year-old Croatian [Milivoj Asner](/wiki/Milivoj_Asner "Milivoj Asner") on its 2005 top ten list. Asner fled to Austria in 2004 after Croatia announced it would start investigations in the case of war crimes he may have been involved in. In response to objections about Asner's continued freedom, Austria's federal government deferred to either extradition requests from Croatia or prosecutorial actions from [Klagenfurt](/wiki/Klagenfurt "Klagenfurt"), claiming reason of dementia in 2008. Milivoj Ašner died on 14 June 2011 at the age of 98 in his room in a [Caritas](/wiki/Caritas_Austria "Caritas Austria") nursing home still in Klagenfurt.

### Sudetenland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anschluss&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Sudetenland")]

The occurrence of the [Sudeten crisis](/wiki/Sudetenland#Sudeten_Crisis "Sudetenland") in early 1938 led to the autumn [Munich Agreement](/wiki/Munich_Agreement "Munich Agreement") after which [Nazi Germany occupied the Sudetenland](/wiki/Sudetenland#Sudetenland_as_part_of_Germany "Sudetenland"). These events taken as a whole can be seen as a mimeograph of the Anschluss page in Hitler's playbook.[[148]](#cite_note-ushmma-152)[[149]](#cite_note-whlfp-153)

## Austrian political and military leaders in Nazi Germany

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Anschluss&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-2)** before the [German orthography reform of 1996](/wiki/German_orthography_reform_of_1996 "German orthography reform of 1996")
2. **[^](#cite_ref-5)** After the [Prussian](/wiki/Prussia "Prussia")-dominated German nation-state was created in 1871 without Austria, the [German question](/wiki/German_question "German question") was still very active in most parts of the ethnic German lands of the [Austro-Hungarian](/wiki/Austria-Hungary "Austria-Hungary") and German empires; the Austrian pan-Germans were in favour of a Pan-German vision of Austria joining Germany in order to create a "Greater Germany" and the Germans inside the German Empire were in favour of all Germans being unified into a single state.[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELow19743-4)
3. **[^](#cite_ref-33)** Hitler was an [ethnic German](/wiki/Ethnic_Germans "Ethnic Germans"), but was not a [German citizen](/wiki/German_nationality_law "German nationality law") by birth since he had been born in the Austro-Hungarian empire. He gave up his Austrian citizenship in 1925 and remained stateless for seven years before he became a German citizen in 1932.[[30]](#cite_note-Lemons2005-32)
4. **[^](#cite_ref-69)** [Albert Speer](/wiki/Albert_Speer "Albert Speer") recalled the Austrians cheering approval as cars of Germans entered what had once been an independent Austria.[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpeer1997109-68)
